title: Lisa May Ceramics Website
text:
Quarto-based portfolio site for handmade ceramics.



pieces/ - Individual piece pages (.qmd files)
images/ - All images (JPG preferred, 1000px long edge currently, moving to 1600px)
mugs.qmd, etc. - Category listing pages





Categories: available for purchase, work in progress, sculpture, mugs, crows, whales, sgraffito, etc.
work in progress items should have draft: true (don’t publish unfinished pieces)
For sale items include “Available for Purchase”, “Price: $X”, and “Volume: X mL (X oz)” at top of content

Each line needs two trailing spaces for markdown line breaks (including Price line)
Format: Available for Purchase␣␣ / Price: $X␣␣ / Volume: X mL (X oz)

Alt text uses fig-alt attribute (not bracket syntax) to avoid visible captions
Lightbox galleries use group="name" attribute for navigation between images
Piece page pattern: hero image at top, then layout-ncol=2 or layout-ncol=3 grid for additional views
For 4 total images: use layout-ncol=3 (1 hero + 3 in one row)
For 5+ images: use layout-ncol=2 (1 hero + 2x2 grid)
Reference/meme images: save locally, display side-by-side with layout-ncol=2, add credit line at bottom in italics
Mugs include: “Durable stoneware, dishwasher and microwave safe.” before Process notes
Cropped detail images for callouts go outside lightbox group (no group= attribute)
Links between piece pages use .html extension (e.g., [sketchy crows](sketchy_crows.html))





All images have copyright: “© Lisa May”
Artist/Creator: “Lisa May”
Copyright Notice: “© Lisa May - lisamayceramics.com”
GPS and device info stripped for privacy



exiftool -overwrite_original \
  '-GPS*=' \
  -Make= \
  -Model= \
  -Software= \
  -SerialNumber= \
  -LensInfo= \
  -LensModel= \
  -LensSerialNumber= \
  -Copyright="© Lisa May" \
  -Artist="Lisa May" \
  -Creator="Lisa May" \
  -CopyrightNotice="© Lisa May - lisamayceramics.com" \
  images/*.jpg images/*.png





Web research is authorized (viewing websites for pricing, technique info, etc.)




Lisa May - lisamayceramics.com

title: Lisa May Ceramics
text:
Artist Manifesto Draft
I am a researcher of form and a translator of the internal landscape. My work is not a product; it is a physical manifestation of a decades-long inquiry into biology, behavior, and the queer, ‘wild’ self. I reject the pressure of production for the depth of the study. My value lies at the intersection of my PhD intellect and my artist hands. I do not merely sell objects; I place artifacts with those who resonate with the narrative. I honor the slow burn, the five-foot risks, and the mental space that belongs entirely to me. I am the Primary Investigator of my own joy.

title: Lisa May Ceramics
text:
About Lisa May (Revised)

From Synapses to Silica: Mapping the Social Landscape
For twenty years, my work focused on the invisible mechanics of connection. As a social neuroscientist with a PhD focused on motivation and decision-making, I spent my career dissecting how biological systems implement social behavior. I was a researcher of the “we”—mapping how individuals synchronize, cooperate, and find meaning within a group.
Now, I build that social architecture in clay.
My transition from the lab to the kiln wasn’t a departure; it was a shift in medium. I’ve traded the abstract study of the brain for the tactile reality of ceramics, but the inquiry remains: How do we inhabit our social worlds?


The Work: Social Specially and Narrative Artifacts
My practice is an ethnographic study of non-human social worlds, translated into 5-foot ceramic forms and storytelling objects.

The Humpback Whales: These pieces are studies in deep-time sociality. To me, a whale is a monument to collective memory, migration, and the “slow pulse” of communal existence. They represent a social intelligence that operates on a scale humans are only beginning to grasp.

The Crows: I am captivated by corvid intelligence—their facial recognition, their “funerals,” and their agile, observational social fabric. My crows are the “street-smart” observers of the studio, representing the sharp, reactive, and highly communicative side of social neuroscience.

The Narrative Objects: I view my functional work (mugs and plates) as “thought-containers.” They are etched with the internal monologues that occur when we are alone, yet still profoundly shaped by our social connections.



The Philosophy
I live at the intersection of colorful glasses, queer identity, and complex data. My studio is my laboratory, where I explore the friction between my scientific training as a Social Neuroscientist and my “wild self.”
I am not interested in production; I am interested in intellectual rigor and artistic discovery. Every piece I fire is an invitation to the viewer to step out of the “optimized” world and into a more interesting, idiosyncratic mental space.
“Here, think my thoughts.”


The Field Note Framework: Decoupling the Inquiry from the Artifact
1. The Primary Work is the Inquiry My true creative output is the intellectual synthesis of social neuroscience, queer identity, and biological form. The writing is not “marketing”—it is the documentation of a formal study. Whether I am observing the gular pouch of a crow or the collective memory of a whale pod, the thought is the first product.
2. The Ceramics are “Specimens,” Not Products The mugs, plates, and 5-foot sculptures are physical artifacts resulting from the inquiry. They are the “data points” I’ve pulled from the kiln. Some data points are clean (a perfect glaze); some are messy (a kiln fail). But a messy data point does not invalidate the research.
3. Protect the “Mental Space” from the “Kiln Gods” By publishing my “Field Notes” independently of the final objects, I protect my intellectual labor from the volatility of the ceramic process. I allow myself to be a storyteller and a scientist first, and a manufacturer second. This removes the “transactional anxiety” and ensures that my “wild self” always has a voice, even when the clay misbehaves.
4. The Value Proposition High-value collectors aren’t just buying a 5-foot sculpture; they are buying the five years of documented “Field Notes” that led to it. The writing builds the provenance of depth that justifies the transition from “maker” to “master.”

The Mantra: The thought is the study; the clay is the evidence. I am the Primary Investigator, and the research stands on its own.

title: The Ideal Mug
text:
At a work dinner recently, I got into a conversation about the ideal mug.
I started this topic because I’ve really been obsessing over it — making the perfect mug. But I realized talking to my colleague that what I’ve been thinking of as the platonic ideal perfect mug is actually just my perfect mug. Big. Chunky. A handle you can really grab — so I can fill it with tea and wander my yard, check on my plants, talk to the crows. Something that says go outside, this is sturdy, honestly if you drop it it’ll be fine — go be wild.
His perfect mug is very much not that. His isn’t really a mug at all. Small. No handle. If it’s too hot, he waits. A thin rim. Although there was something we shared — texture. It should feel good in your hands.
I loved this. Not the answer itself, but the seriousness of it. The way someone can have a whole philosophy about a small thing they hold every morning. These tiny details I’ve been turning over for months — he had his own version, and it was almost the opposite of mine.
So I made his mug. Or I tried to.

I started with a basic cylinder. Simple. TINY. I didn’t even have a form to use — I searched through my pantry for something the right size. Ten minutes of work if I’d left it alone.

But I didn’t leave it alone, because carving is the best part. It’s the chaos demon — the thing I do because it feels good, not because it’s in the plan. I carved vertical lines into it, and then I kept going.

Carved holes. Was content patching them. Put a big coil on the outside, wrapped it up in its little blanket, then carved again. Carved more holes. The whole lid came off.

So I put a coil on the inside and pressed it back together. Two hours on something that started as a ten-minute form.
There’s always this tension for me: the planned thing versus the release of just doing something because it feels good. Making something beautiful versus making something interesting. Usually I lean toward chunky, substantial, heavy in the hand. But this piece ended up thin. Really thin. The rim is thin. The walls are thin. And that surprised me, because that’s not how I usually work. But it makes sense. This isn’t my mug. This was shaped by a different person’s ideal — someone more refined, someone who waits for the coffee to cool.

What happens next: the inside is a little rough, but I might leave it. If it survives the bisque, I’ll sand it down. I’m thinking a simple glaze — something that lets the carved texture show through, that breaks nicely over the ridges. Something you can feel.

title: About
text:
I have a PhD in Neuroscience from the University of Oregon and work as a Workflow Coach for Posit Software.
I tend to find the social lives of non-human animals interesting - thus crows and whales as subject matter.

title: Stars & Mountains Mug
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $50
Volume: 413 mL (14 oz)














Inspired by looking at a picture of this stars cup, I made this! For these, I always need the mountains/land/horizon to be waaaayyy down on the mug, because I’m chasing that feeling of laying in a field as a kid in the plains of Montana, with the sky a bowl above me. BIG SKY.
This mug will do you right. She’s good to hold, she’s a good size (14 oz/413 mL), good vibes.
Durable stoneware, dishwasher and microwave safe.

title: Buckwheat Mug
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $40
Volume: 522 mL (18 oz)














Listen, don’t tell the rest of the mugs, but right out of the kiln this is one of the best mugs of its cohort.
It’s big, it’s strong, it’s a simple mug. It’s not an artist, it might not be interesting with crows or such, but it doesn’t need all that. It’s perfect as it is.
Also obsessed with this buckwheat glaze. On a test tile I was all “boring, when would I ever want that?”
But for this it’s just right. Look at how it breaks over the carving, it’s eeeeiiii so good.

title: Pod of Whales
text:
These whale were in my mind for a long time.


I used to work in an office space with a super high ceiling, and I had this image of the ocean and the different biological niches. You know, the sunlight zone, the twilight zone… we were the bottom dwellers in this tall tall space and there could be different biological niches above us and we’d just be oblivious to it.




 


 



In real life, it can feel like your entire biological niche changes when you change your social circle. Like…changing your job or go through a breakup…like you live in the same town but never see all the people you used to see every day. Or you start a new hobby or make a new friend and that gives you a whole new social world. All of these lives are going on around us and it doesn’t take much to make the world feel totally different.










The feeling of the different whales relating to one another— they’re playing, they’re dancing, they’re in their bodies and joyful. Movement. A sense of movement and connectedness. What must it feel like to dive like that? To be that powerful of a swimmer?

title: Doors Mug
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $50
Volume: 577 mL (20 oz)














This is a big, bold mug — 20 oz (577 mL) — for someone who wants to commit to their coffee.
The design is based on medieval cathedral doors. I’ve been thinking about doors lately as points of transition, especially house doors. The transition between what’s internal and private vs. external and public. How we navigate those choices. Doors to different futures, possible choices, other dimensions.
This piece marks a turning point for me: it’s where I started treating mugs as tiny canvases, sketching out a composition based on the space.

title: Sketchy Crows Mug
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $50
Volume: 182 mL (6 oz)
























She’s a small mug (6 oz/182 mL), for a Sunday morning when you’re close to the coffee pot or have a pot of tea brewing. Or maybe you’re just an elegant person who drinks in small volumes.
This crow from behind is my favorite. Mysterious. 
Then she turns and makes awkward eye contact. Whut? 
This is the least successful carving although the one I love the most from the reference sketch. I can’t get that head tilt right yet! 
This mug marks a turning point based on a fluid sketch, less like a plan with very clear clean outlines, less like a cartoon, less controlled. I like stuff that has a weird messy energy.
Carving clay feels amazing. Sometimes I crave it (not making that up). I loved the chaos of carving those horizontal stripes. Chaos gremlin release!
Durable stoneware, dishwasher and microwave safe.
Process notes
This is the reference sketch I made to work from. 
And here’s an example of the reference pictures I’m taking of MY CROWS lately because they trust me and come when I whistle and come very close to me. Squeeeeeeeeeeee.

title: Mark Making Mug
text:
This mug, she shines bright. Guaranteed good vibes with bright orange and turquoise. It gives me the feeling of a herd of animals moving across a plain or tropical fish in a coral reef. Collective life, moving together, vibrant color.
It’s a great mug for holding, the texture is very satisfying.
Durable stoneware, dishwasher and microwave safe.
Process notes
I woke up in the middle of the night to carve this. Each mark is hand carved. Small repetitive hand movements are so soothing. Then it was bisque fired and I added the turquoise, wiped it back so only the blue stayed in the marks, and added clear glaze.

title: Singing Whale
text: Sigh. I really loved this whale, but I screwed up the glazing. So we’ll just say that I learned a lot. Maybe someday I’ll try to reglaze and fire.

title: Holy Whale
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $225
Male humpbacks sing for hours, spiraling slowly as their voices carry for miles. This sculpture holds that moment — peaceful, embodied, part of the great chorus.




































Hand sculpted in stoneware for lasting durability, indoors or out. Dimensions: 16” × 8” × 4” (41 × 20 × 10 cm). Two mounting holes allow for easy hanging with nails, screws, or wire.
Stoneware goes through two firings. Here it is after sculpting, before the first fire — and a glimpse of the hollowing process that makes large pieces possible.

title: Crow Babies
text:
Available for Purchase Price: $45
Email may.lisa2@gmail.com to inquire.

This summer I watched a juvenile crow beg for food while she was standing on food. The begging went on for days. Man, oh man, it really made me empathize with the parents. And crows do it every year! This a set of four small plates is an ode to parental patience and how hard it is to raise kids. :) They’re little plates - perfect for dunking chicken nuggets in ketchup or holding your rings.

title: Golden Green Mug
text:
Available for Purchase
Price: $50
Volume: 248 mL (8 oz)



















This is a smaller mug (8 oz / 248 mL), and it’s wierd, wonderful, and one-of-a-kind. This glaze is called Golden Green and I’m falling in love with the weird olive green/yellow.
This is based on drawings from my crows in my backyard, a sister to sketchy crows.
